steps.annotation: Define a function named greet. Body holds reusable code.
Body — runs when greet() is called from anywhere.
main calls greet by name + parens. Control jumps into greet body.
One named block — call as many times as you want.
First use. Prints --- via the function body.
Reuse — no copy/paste of cout. That's the point of functions.
Function bundles 3 cout statements under one name.
One call runs all 3 statements in order.
Define hello above main so the compiler sees it before any call.
main is the entry point — execution starts here.
Call hello. Control jumps up into the function body.

teachMe: main calls hi() which runs cout << hi. Then return ends the program.
Three calls to line() = three dashes. Same body re-used.
Function bundles two cout statements; one call runs both.
After greet() returns, control resumes at line 5 in main.
Two separate functions. main calls them in order.
Loop calls box() three times — each call prints [].
main calls main2 which calls hi. Chain: main -> main2 -> hi.

clozeSentence: Use ___ when the function returns no value
explanation: void means no return value — the function does work without producing one.

clozeSentence: Function body opens with ___
explanation: Body of any function is enclosed in { }.

spec: Write a function named line that takes no arguments, returns nothing, and prints --- followed by endl.
explanation: void = no return; () = no params; body has the cout statement.

spec: Write a function named scoreboard that takes no args, returns nothing, and prints two lines: 'Home: 0' and 'Away: 0'.
explanation: Function body holds the two print statements; one call runs both.

steps.annotation: Signature: returnType int, name add, params (int a, int b).
Body uses params and returns a value matching the type.
Caller must match the signature: 2 ints in, 1 int out.
void return + 1 param.
int return + 1 int param.
double return + 1 double param. Same shape, different types.
() means no parameters. Empty parens still required.
int return: must produce an int.
Two params separated by comma; each needs its own type.
Caller passes two doubles in matching order.

teachMe: Caller matches signature: 2 ints in, 1 int out. Returns 3+4 = 7.
Signature int square(int) matches call square(5). Returns 5*5=25.
Call passes 10 and 3 in order. Returns 10-3 = 7.
double type matches. 7.0/2.0 = 3.5.
n=8 in body. Returns 8*3 = 24.
Three params, three args, in matching order: 1+2+3 = 6.
No-arg signature (). Always returns 42.

clozeSentence: Each param needs its own type. Second param: ___
explanation: Both params need their own type — you cannot share like 'int a, b'.

clozeSentence: No return value -> use ___ as return type
explanation: void marks the function as returning nothing.

spec: Write the function header for an int-returning function named area that takes int width and int height (no body).
explanation: Signature shape: returnType name(paramList) — matches int area(int, int).

spec: Write the function header for a double-returning function named average that takes double total and int count.
explanation: Each param has its own type: (double total, int count).

steps.annotation: name(args) is the call. Args 3 and 4 fill params a and b.
Returned value lives in sum. Print it.
Call returns a value — used directly as cout argument.
Calls also work in expressions: 9 + 1 = 10.
void calls become standalone statements ending in ;.
Args fill params left-to-right: a=10, b=3 -> 7.
Reverse order: a=3, b=10 -> -7. Order changes meaning.

teachMe: Call add(3,4) returns 7. Stored in s. Print 7.
Call returns 21 used directly in cout.
square(2)=4 + square(3)=9 -> 13.
Order matters: a=3, b=10 -> 3-10 = -7.
Inner call dbl(3) = 6. Outer dbl(6) = 12.
a = inc(5) = 6. b = inc(6) = 7. Print 7.
void call is a statement. Prints go then !.

clozeSentence: Call provides arguments inside parens: ___
explanation: name(args) — args inside (). Order matches param order.

clozeSentence: Even with no args, call still uses ___
explanation: Call needs () even when no args. Without () it's not a call.

spec: Given int add(int a, int b), call it with 5 and 9 inside cout to print the result.
explanation: Call returns int; cout uses returned value directly.

steps.annotation: x is a fresh local box inside f, separate from caller's variable.
Assign 99 to LOCAL x. Caller's n is untouched.
n is still 5 in main — params get a copy by default.
x copies into param a; y copies into param b.
Inside, a and b are local boxes holding the copies.
Param n exists only inside show's body.
7 fills n. After show returns, n is destroyed.

levelLabel: L8 -> changing param doesn't affect caller

steps.annotation: v is the local copy. Setting v doesn't change balance.
Prints 500 — caller's balance untouched (no & in param).

teachMe: f's x is a copy. Assign in f doesn't change main's n. Prints 5.
grow's v is local; v+=10 only changes the copy. score stays 50.
Inside dbl, n becomes 14. Returns 14 -> y. But x in main stays 7.
Args 3,5 fill params a,b. cout prints 3+5 = 8.
Swap inside rotate touches local x,y only. main's a,b stay 1,2.
Each inc gets a fresh copy in v. counter never changes — prints 10.
v=4 is copied into param n. n*n = 16 returned.

clozeSentence: Params behave like ___ variables of the function
explanation: Each param is a local box; changes don't escape unless & is used.

clozeSentence: Use ___ as the first arg to fill param a
explanation: Args fill params left to right: n -> a, 4 -> b.

spec: Write a void function named bump that takes int v and increments v by 1. (No effect on caller — just demonstrates locals.)
explanation: Without &, v is local. Caller's variable unaffected.

steps.annotation: return ENDS the function and hands back the value to the caller.
Returned 16 lands in s.
Early return — function exits HERE if n is negative. Skips line 3.
Only reached when n is non-negative.
Function gives a value back; caller uses it in math.
Returned 5.0 added to 50.0 -> total = 55.0.
Declared int return — must return an int (n/2 is int division).
Declared double return — must return a double (n/2.0 stays double).

teachMe: return n*n hands 36 back. Used directly in cout.
abs_val(-7): early return -(-7)=7. abs_val(4): falls through to return 4.
8>3 true so early return 8. Skip second return.
Both ifs false. Falls to return 0.
First call returns 5; second returns 5; total 10.
int return: 7/2 = 3 (integer division).
After return, code below is dead. cout never runs. Returns 5.

clozeSentence: Send the value back with ___
explanation: return ends the function and reports a value to the caller.

clozeSentence: return statements end with ___
explanation: return is a statement; needs ; to terminate.

spec: Write a function int square(int n) that returns n*n.
explanation: int return type, int param, body returns the squared value.

spec: Write a function double tax(double price) that returns price * 0.1.
explanation: double return type matches the body's float multiplication.

prompt: Write a function int dbl(int n) that returns n*2.

variants.prompt: Write int dbl(int n) returning twice its arg.
Function dbl that doubles an int and returns it.

steps.annotation: void = no return value. Function does work but reports nothing.
Body has side effects (prints) but no return statement.
Call as a statement — can't put it in cout because no value.

levelLabel: L8 -> void can use bare return

steps.annotation: void function can return; (no value) to exit early.
Only reached when n>=0.
void: prints but doesn't return — used for its side effect.
int: returns a value the caller can use.
void function for side effect (logging) — no value to return.
Call as statement; can't be used as a value.

teachMe: void function called twice prints x twice.
First call returns early (no print). Second prints 7.
void log called twice. Note 23.0 prints as 23 by default.
void with multiple statements — all run on each call.
void prints n*2 = 10. No return value.
void with string param. Concatenates greeting.
Bare return; ends void function. Code after never runs.

clozeSentence: Function returns nothing -> use ___
explanation: void marks no return value.

clozeSentence: Bare ___ exits void early without a value
explanation: return; (no value) ends a void function early.

spec: Write a void function named log_msg that takes a string m and prints it followed by endl.
explanation: void = no return; one string param; body prints + endl.

spec: Write a void function named pulse that takes no args and prints 'beep' three times each on its own line.
explanation: void with no args; body has 3 print statements.

steps.annotation: Define greet ABOVE main so the compiler sees it first.
By line 5, compiler already knows greet exists -> OK.
Prototype: tells compiler the signature so main can call square.
Compiler trusts the prototype — actual body comes later.
Definition can now live below main.
At this point compiler hasn't seen greet — ERROR without prototype.
Definition appears too late; compiler already failed.
Layout A: define before use.
Layout B: prototype above use, define below.

teachMe: Definition above main -> compiler sees hi before the call.
Prototype on line 1 lets main call square. Definition follows below.
Prototype + definition both above main — both legal patterns.
Function defined first, then main calls it. Returns 14.
b uses a — must be defined first. main calls b. Output: B then A.
Prototype with type-only param OK. half(10) returns 5.
Inner chain(3)=4. Outer chain(4)=5.

clozeSentence: Without a prototype above main, the compiler errors. Type returnType: ___
explanation: Without a prototype, you must define before use OR add a prototype.

clozeSentence: A prototype ends with ___
explanation: Prototype is a declaration, not a definition — ; not { }.

spec: Write the prototype for an int-returning function named area that takes int width and int height.
explanation: Prototype matches signature exactly, ends with ; (no body).

steps.annotation: Prototype: returnType name(types); — types only, no names needed.
void return + 1 string param.
Each type listed; ends with ; not { body }.

fullCode:
int add(int a, int b);   // names allowed but optional
int add(int, int);       // also valid

steps.annotation: Prototype CAN include param names — improves readability.
Names are optional in prototypes — only types matter.
Prototype declares signature without body.
Definition has a body and matching signature.

levelLabel: L8 -> prototype no semicolon = body required

steps.annotation: ; means it's a declaration only — the body is elsewhere.
{ means it's a definition — body follows.

teachMe: Prototype lets main call sq. Definition below provides body. 4*4=16.
Prototype with type-only param. Definition supplies the name.
Prototype lists types only; definition names them. Returns 5.
Prototype + definition both above main = legal. Returns 21.
Prototype declares double half(double). 9.0/2.0 = 4.5.
Two prototypes group together. Definitions follow below main.
Prototype with names matches definition exactly. 10-4=6.

code: int square(int n)___
clozeSentence: A prototype ends with ___ (not body)
explanation: Prototype declaration ends with ; — no { } body.

code: void greet(___);  // type-only prototype
clozeSentence: Prototype param can be type-only: ___
explanation: Prototypes need types, names are optional.

spec: Write a prototype for double avg(int total, int count). Type-only style is fine.
explanation: Prototype: returnType name(types); — body comes later.

spec: Write a prototype for int find(string name).
explanation: Prototype with names is fine; ends with ; not body.

question: Write the canonical prototype for int square(int n).
code: int square(int n);

steps.annotation: Local box created when f starts.

steps.code: // local dies HERE when f returns
steps.annotation: Frame popped at return — local box destroyed.
Fresh n each call. Previous values are gone.
Always prints 1 — n doesn't persist between calls.
x is a local box made from arg copy.
Param shares the function frame — gone at return.
v becomes 0 inside zero.
score is still 99 — local v's change was lost.

teachMe: Local n exists only inside f. Prints 5 then dies at return.
Each call gets fresh n=0. Prints 1 every time, never accumulates.
f's local x becomes 107. After return x dies. main's v stays 7.
Each call resets c to 0. Always returns 1. Prints 111.
v becomes 0 then dies. score never changed. Prints 99.
local dies after return — but its VALUE 42 was already copied to v.
Inner block has its own t=99 then dies. Outer t=1 returns.

clozeSentence: Closing ___ ends the function frame; locals die
explanation: } closes the function body — frame popped, locals gone.

clozeSentence: Function locals are inaccessible to caller — unseen ___
explanation: Local n exists only inside f's frame; outside it's not visible.

spec: Write a void function named bump that creates int counter = 0, increments it, and prints it. Show that locals don't persist by saying so in a comment.
explanation: Locals reset every call — counter never accumulates.

prompt: Write a void function trial() that declares int x = 1, prints x, and returns. (Demonstrates a local that dies on return.)

variants.prompt: void trial(): int x=1, cout x.
Function trial with local int x=1 that prints then returns.

question: Write a void function f with a local int n = 5 that prints n.

steps.annotation: secret lives only in f's frame.
Outside f, name secret doesn't exist — compiler error.
return copies v's VALUE out before v dies.
main never saw v itself — only the value 10.
internal is fully encapsulated.
Function is a black box — outside cannot reach in.

levelLabel: L8 -> two functions can't share locals

steps.annotation: x exists only inside a's frame.
Different function frame — b cannot see a's x.

teachMe: v's value 10 escapes via return. main never named v.
f prints its own local. main never sees hidden.
f's local x=1 shadows global. Global x=100 is what main prints.
inner's n is private. outer cannot read it. Only inner prints 5.
Locals total + extra invisible to main. Only the sum 57 escapes.
local computed and returned; main only sees the returned value 12.
a and b each have their own x. Frames isolated. Outputs 1 and 99.

clozeSentence: Cannot access hidden ___ from outside f
explanation: Locals are private — only return or & exposes them.

clozeSentence: Use ___ to send v's value out
explanation: return is one way to expose a local's value to the caller.

spec: Write a function int give_secret() that creates int code = 1234, then returns it. The local code dies but its value escapes via return.
explanation: Local code dies but return copies its value to caller.

prompt: Write a function int give() with a local int v = 10 that returns v.

variants.prompt: int give() that has int v=10 and returns v.
Write a function with a local v=10 and return it.

question: Write a function int give() that returns a local int v = 10.
